body_txt: The stereotypical opening to a tabletop RPG adventure: the protagonists are all gathered by prior intent or a "coincidence" of Game Master fiat, in an inn, bar room, or other common public meeting spot. Once there, some Quest Giver NPC will approach them with a job offer or plea for assistance. These strangers tend to seat themselves in the darkest corner of the tavern (probably to seem more mysterious, or following that famous forerunner, Strider .) Thus do our heroes receive their ticket to board the plot . Careful, though, for the mysterious stranger has a fifty-fifty chance of being a Treacherous Quest Giver , maybe even the Big Bad in disguise. If the PCs get rowdy or the GM just wants to test out combat, a Bar Brawl may occur. Fortunately, the barkeep is usually a retired former adventurer willing and able to kick the asses of any troublemakers. This trope is Older Than Print — no less an author than Chaucer had his traveling party meet in an inn — but it later began to be considered a Discredited Trope through overuse. Actually starting an adventure with the words "So, you all meet in an inn..." may be seen as roleplaying's equivalent to " It Was a Dark and Stormy Night ..." Thus, a lot of sources advise against using it, and list alternative ideas for beginnings. The 3rd Edition Dungeon Master's Guide for Dungeons & Dragons , in a list of ways to bring a party together, dubs this "The Cliche". David Morgan-Mar, of Irregular Webcomic! and Darths & Droids fame, provides a list of less overused ways to start an adventure, as do the folks at the Dice of Doom blog. On the other hand, cliched as it may be, it does have some logic behind it. Taverns are the center of social life in many cultures, making for a good place to meet new people, and food and drink are good for bonding with new acquaintances. Additionally, from a roleplay perspective, a tavern scene hits a sweet spot: it requires no greater explanation for why the characters are there than "they wanted food or drink", provides a peaceful setting for the characters to get familiar with each other and establish their identities, and has just enough things going on (ordering drinks, interacting with the staff or other customers, singing songs, brooding in the corner, bar-fights) to give the players something to do before the GM hooks them into the plot. Some people use the trope deliberately as an invocation of gaming tradition. It's also quite easy to play for laughs, emphasizing the comedy potential of downing a few pints and then deciding to go out and slay a dragon with your new-found acquaintances. Real Life group meetings at an inn are usually a convention of some description. The "adventuring" usually takes place entirely within the building, and does not normally involve bloodshed, swordplay or dragons. Unless it's a gaming convention, or maybe for fencing, or blood donation. Sister Trope of You All Meet in a Cell (the story begins with a group of protagonists in captivity) and Adventure Guild (a building that provides adventurers with quests as an explicit service ). Contrast Closed Circle , for when the DM wants to keep you in the tavern. Compare and contrast Hero Looking for Group (the main protagonist actively seeks teammates to accompany her in her mission), as well as Putting the Band Together .
